stage.name: OpenHW foundations: industrial open-source RISC-V IP
stage.description: A detailed OpenHW Explorer classroom built from OpenHW Group public slide decks on commercial adoption, CORE-V deliverables, licensing, verification, ecosystem, and digital sovereignty.
stage.courseTemplateName: Professional Technical Deep Dive - Evidence-Grounded SlideKit

scenes.title: OpenHW foundations: industrial open-source RISC-V IP

content.title: OpenHW foundations: industrial open-source RISC-V IP
content.subtitle: A detailed classroom introduction built from two OpenHW Group public slide decks: 2023 commercial adoption lessons and 2024 HiPEAC industrial-use overview.
content.thesis: OpenHW is not just a collection of repositories. It is an attempt to make open-source processor IP usable by real companies: legally understandable, technically verifiable, ecosystem-backed, and teachable.

sourceAnchors.title: HiPEAC 2024 page 7: OpenHW and RISC-V
sourceAnchors.claimSupported: OpenHW is presented as a RISC-V International member and an organization that makes industrial-quality open-source RISC-V core implementations available.

sourceAnchors.title: Lessons Learned 2023 page 14: adoption lessons
sourceAnchors.claimSupported: The 2023 deck frames commercial open-source hardware adoption through permissive use, IP quality, and roadmap/ecosystem readiness.

content.speakerNotes: In this lesson we are not going to memorize every OpenHW project name. We will build a usable mental model. OpenHW sits where open RISC-V ideas have to become implementation-quality IP. That means licenses must be commercially acceptable, verification has to look familiar to industry teams, and the surrounding software, boards, documentation, and roadmap must make adoption feel realistic instead of heroic.

actions.text: In this lesson we are not going to memorize every OpenHW project name. We will build a usable mental model. OpenHW sits where open RISC-V ideas have to become implementation-quality IP. That means licenses must be commercially acceptable, verification has to look familiar to industry teams, and the surrounding software, boards, documentation, and roadmap must make adoption feel realistic instead of heroic.

scenes.title: RISC-V is the ISA; OpenHW turns it into usable IP

content.title: RISC-V is the ISA; OpenHW turns it into usable IP
content.subtitle: The first distinction keeps the whole course clean: an instruction set is not the same thing as a core implementation.

columns.label: RISC-V
columns.title: The specification layer
bullets: Defines the instruction-set architecture and related standards.
Enables a shared target for tools, software, compliance, and ecosystem discussion.
Does not by itself give a company a production-ready processor core.

columns.label: OpenHW
columns.title: The implementation layer
bullets: Organizes open-source RISC-V core families such as CVE2, CVE4, and CVA6.
Adds verification, documentation, toolchain, board, and SoC-oriented deliverables.
Creates a neutral place where members can collaborate on reusable processor IP.

sourceAnchors.title: HiPEAC 2024 page 7: OpenHW and RISC-V
sourceAnchors.claimSupported: OpenHW is presented as a RISC-V International member and an organization that makes industrial-quality open-source RISC-V core implementations available.

sourceAnchors.title: HiPEAC 2024 page 12: OpenHW deliverables
sourceAnchors.claimSupported: OpenHW deliverables include CORE-V core families, verification, documentation, compilers, RTOS/bare-metal/Linux support, FPGA images, SoCs, and commercial EDA tool compatibility.

content.speakerNotes: A clean way to explain the stack is this: RISC-V tells you what the machine must mean; OpenHW works on implementations that engineers can actually inspect, verify, modify, and integrate. This matters because a company adopting open hardware does not only ask, 'Is the ISA open?' It asks, 'Where is the core, the testbench, the documentation, the compiler path, and the board-level proof that this can become a product?'

actions.text: A clean way to explain the stack is this: RISC-V tells you what the machine must mean; OpenHW works on implementations that engineers can actually inspect, verify, modify, and integrate. This matters because a company adopting open hardware does not only ask, 'Is the ISA open?' It asks, 'Where is the core, the testbench, the documentation, the compiler path, and the board-level proof that this can become a product?'

scenes.title: The deliverable is an ecosystem, not a lonely RTL folder

content.title: The deliverable is an ecosystem, not a lonely RTL folder
content.subtitle: The HiPEAC material frames OpenHW around core families surrounded by verification, software, documentation, boards, SoCs, and tools.
content.body: A serious open-source hardware project must lower the integration cost around the core. The surrounding rings are not decoration; they are what make the RTL credible to a product team.
content.caption: OpenHW Group deliverables slide, HiPEAC 2024, page 12.

callouts.title: Core families
callouts.body: CVE2, CVE4, and CVA6 are presented as the central CORE-V RISC-V core families.

callouts.title: Industrial tooling
callouts.body: The material explicitly names SystemVerilog, UVM, Questa, Cadence, Synopsys, Imperas, LLVM/GCC, Linux, RTOS, and FPGA images.

assets.caption: HiPEAC 2024 page 12: OpenHW deliverables around CORE-V core families.

sourceAnchors.title: HiPEAC 2024 page 12: OpenHW deliverables
sourceAnchors.claimSupported: OpenHW deliverables include CORE-V core families, verification, documentation, compilers, RTOS/bare-metal/Linux support, FPGA images, SoCs, and commercial EDA tool compatibility.

content.speakerNotes: This slide is one of the most important ones in the whole source set. It says, in visual form, that OpenHW is not trying to win by publishing RTL alone. Around the core there must be UVM testbenches, SystemVerilog design source, documentation, compilers, operating-system paths, and board or SoC artifacts. That is exactly the difference between a demo repository and something an industrial team can plan around.

actions.text: This slide is one of the most important ones in the whole source set. It says, in visual form, that OpenHW is not trying to win by publishing RTL alone. Around the core there must be UVM testbenches, SystemVerilog design source, documentation, compilers, operating-system paths, and board or SoC artifacts. That is exactly the difference between a demo repository and something an industrial team can plan around.

scenes.title: A practical map of the CORE-V family

content.title: A practical map of the CORE-V family
content.subtitle: For a learner, the roadmap is less about memorizing names and more about understanding why different cores exist.

cards.label: CVE2
cards.title: Small embedded baseline
cards.body: A compact entry point for microcontroller-class work, useful when the question is about small embedded control rather than application-class processing.

cards.label: CVE4
cards.title: Embedded performance family
cards.body: A family aimed at richer embedded use cases, where performance, interrupt behavior, DSP-style options, and MCU platform work start to matter more.

cards.label: CVA6
cards.title: Application-class reading path
cards.body: The family that becomes central in OpenHW Explorer because it exposes MMU, Linux-capable integration concerns, deeper microarchitecture, and system-level boundaries.

sourceAnchors.title: HiPEAC 2024 page 13: OpenHW RISC-V roadmap
sourceAnchors.claimSupported: The roadmap maps CVE2, CVE4, CVA6, CVW, and planned variants across performance classes, while noting that the roadmap is member-driven and may change.

sourceAnchors.title: Lessons Learned 2023 page 4: CORE-V CVA6 platform
sourceAnchors.claimSupported: The CVA6 platform example shows a vendor-neutral open-source RISC-V system platform with reusable CVA6 instances, memory system, peripherals, and open-source hardware/software stack expectations.

content.speakerNotes: The core names become less intimidating once you read them as a product map. CVE2 is where a compact embedded baseline lives. CVE4 moves toward richer embedded cores and MCU use cases. CVA6 is the application-class path where memory management, Linux-capable integration, cache and interface boundaries, and deeper source reading become unavoidable. That is why CVA6 deserves its own long course series.

actions.text: The core names become less intimidating once you read them as a product map. CVE2 is where a compact embedded baseline lives. CVE4 moves toward richer embedded cores and MCU use cases. CVA6 is the application-class path where memory management, Linux-capable integration, cache and interface boundaries, and deeper source reading become unavoidable. That is why CVA6 deserves its own long course series.

scenes.title: CVA6 shows why platform thinking matters

content.title: CVA6 shows why platform thinking matters
content.subtitle: The 2023 adoption deck uses a CVA6 platform slide to show how a core becomes part of a larger system story.
content.body: The platform picture is valuable because it moves the learner from 'a processor core exists' to 'what must surround this core for a reusable system to exist?'
content.caption: CORE-V CVA6 platform slide, commercial adoption lessons deck, November 2023, page 4.

callouts.title: Core instance reuse
callouts.body: The slide references dual and octal CVA6 platform possibilities, which is a strong signal that reuse and scaling are part of the story.

callouts.title: System boundary
callouts.body: Memory system, IO, and platform components remind us that a core is not a complete SoC by itself.

assets.caption: Lessons Learned 2023 page 4: CORE-V CVA6 platform example.

sourceAnchors.title: Lessons Learned 2023 page 4: CORE-V CVA6 platform
sourceAnchors.claimSupported: The CVA6 platform example shows a vendor-neutral open-source RISC-V system platform with reusable CVA6 instances, memory system, peripherals, and open-source hardware/software stack expectations.

content.speakerNotes: This is where CVA6 becomes more than a name in a repository. The platform slide shows CVA6 instances in a wider system context: memory, IO, boot and software assumptions, and vendor-neutral reuse. The lesson for us is practical. When we teach CVA6, we should never pretend the core alone is the whole product. The engineering value appears when the core boundary and platform boundary are both visible.

actions.text: This is where CVA6 becomes more than a name in a repository. The platform slide shows CVA6 instances in a wider system context: memory, IO, boot and software assumptions, and vendor-neutral reuse. The lesson for us is practical. When we teach CVA6, we should never pretend the core alone is the whole product. The engineering value appears when the core boundary and platform boundary are both visible.

scenes.title: The adoption argument has three gates

content.title: The adoption argument has three gates
content.subtitle: The 2023 deck reduces commercial open-source hardware adoption into three surprisingly disciplined questions.

items.title: Gate 1: permissive use
items.body: Can companies adopt, modify, ship, and contribute without introducing unacceptable legal or patent risk?

items.title: Gate 2: IP quality
items.body: Does the project use design and verification habits that experienced hardware teams can trust?

items.title: Gate 3: roadmap and ecosystem
items.body: Are software, boards, operating systems, physical-design paths, and future core options visible enough to justify investment?

items.title: The hidden gate: confidence
items.body: A company needs confidence that the project will not collapse into a one-off research artifact after first evaluation.

sourceAnchors.title: Lessons Learned 2023 page 14: adoption lessons
sourceAnchors.claimSupported: The 2023 deck frames commercial open-source hardware adoption through permissive use, IP quality, and roadmap/ecosystem readiness.

content.speakerNotes: The 2023 deck is useful because it does not romanticize open hardware. It asks three business-facing questions. First, can the company use the IP permissively? Second, is the IP quality high enough to fit a serious verification culture? Third, is there a roadmap and ecosystem around the core? In other words, adoption is not a vibe. It is legal confidence, technical confidence, and planning confidence.

actions.text: The 2023 deck is useful because it does not romanticize open hardware. It asks three business-facing questions. First, can the company use the IP permissively? Second, is the IP quality high enough to fit a serious verification culture? Third, is there a roadmap and ecosystem around the core? In other words, adoption is not a vibe. It is legal confidence, technical confidence, and planning confidence.

scenes.title: Permissive licensing is a product feature

content.title: Permissive licensing is a product feature
content.subtitle: For hardware companies, licensing is not a footnote. It changes whether adoption can pass legal and business review.
content.body: The material points to Apache-style permissive terms and Solderpad as a way to make rights clearer for hardware, open silicon, and gateware.
content.caption: Solderpad Hardware License 2.1 slide, Lessons Learned 2023, page 17.

callouts.title: Why not just enthusiasm?
callouts.body: Large semiconductor companies hold deep patent portfolios, so unclear obligations can block adoption before engineering begins.

callouts.title: Why Solderpad?
callouts.body: The deck presents Solderpad as an Apache-2.0-based hardware-aware permissive license path.

assets.caption: Lessons Learned 2023 page 17: Solderpad Hardware License 2.1.

sourceAnchors.title: Lessons Learned 2023 page 16: hardware companies and open source
sourceAnchors.claimSupported: The deck highlights why hardware companies prefer permissive licensing: patent exposure, commercial benefit, and already accepted Apache-style terms matter for adoption.

sourceAnchors.title: Lessons Learned 2023 page 17: Solderpad Hardware License 2.1
sourceAnchors.claimSupported: Solderpad Hardware License 2.1 is presented as a permissive open hardware license based on Apache-2.0 with explicit hardware, open silicon, and gateware coverage.

content.speakerNotes: For software people, licensing sometimes feels like an appendix. For hardware companies, it can decide whether a project is even evaluated. The OpenHW lesson is that permissive use is not a philosophical decoration; it is a product feature. If a company can modify, integrate, manufacture, and sell with a licensing story it already understands, the technical conversation has a chance to begin.

actions.text: For software people, licensing sometimes feels like an appendix. For hardware companies, it can decide whether a project is even evaluated. The OpenHW lesson is that permissive use is not a philosophical decoration; it is a product feature. If a company can modify, integrate, manufacture, and sell with a licensing story it already understands, the technical conversation has a chance to begin.

scenes.title: IP quality means speaking the language of hardware teams

content.title: IP quality means speaking the language of hardware teams
content.subtitle: Open hardware becomes more credible when it fits into the verification and EDA reality that companies already use.

columns.label: Design
columns.title: Readable, reusable source
bullets: SystemVerilog is a familiar industry language for the core deliverables.
Documentation must help engineers find boundaries, parameters, and integration assumptions.
A serious project avoids hiding critical knowledge in tribal memory.

columns.label: Verification
columns.title: Evidence, not optimism
bullets: UVM environments and regression habits help teams trust changes.
Commercial tools are part of the practical flow, not a failure of open-source ideals.
A core becomes adoptable when its verification story can survive review.

sourceAnchors.title: Lessons Learned 2023 page 19: Verification Task Group
sourceAnchors.claimSupported: The Verification Task Group develops best-in-class testbench environments for cores and IP blocks developed within OpenHW.

sourceAnchors.title: Lessons Learned 2023 page 20: Industry standard tools
sourceAnchors.claimSupported: The deck states that open-source hardware projects should use industry-standard languages and verification methods and fit existing EDA tool flows.

sourceAnchors.title: HiPEAC 2024 page 12: OpenHW deliverables
sourceAnchors.claimSupported: OpenHW deliverables include CORE-V core families, verification, documentation, compilers, RTOS/bare-metal/Linux support, FPGA images, SoCs, and commercial EDA tool compatibility.

content.speakerNotes: The second lesson is about quality, but not quality as a slogan. Open-source hardware has to speak the language of the engineers who will sign off the design. That means SystemVerilog source, UVM-style environments, repeatable regressions, documentation, and compatibility with serious EDA flows. The open-source part lowers access. The quality system lowers fear.

actions.text: The second lesson is about quality, but not quality as a slogan. Open-source hardware has to speak the language of the engineers who will sign off the design. That means SystemVerilog source, UVM-style environments, repeatable regressions, documentation, and compatibility with serious EDA flows. The open-source part lowers access. The quality system lowers fear.

scenes.title: Open-source hardware still has to fit real EDA flows

content.title: Open-source hardware still has to fit real EDA flows
content.subtitle: The source material is explicit: use industry-standard languages and verification methods, and fit existing company tool flows.
content.body: This is a mature position. Open source does not mean rejecting commercial tools when commercial tools are what production teams use for serious verification and implementation.
content.caption: Industry Standard Tools slide, Lessons Learned 2023, page 20.

callouts.title: A pragmatic ecosystem
callouts.body: OpenHW uses open collaboration while acknowledging Questa, Cadence, Synopsys, Imperas, and similar tool realities.

callouts.title: Teaching implication
callouts.body: A good classroom should teach source reading and verification expectations together, not treat them as separate worlds.

assets.caption: Lessons Learned 2023 page 20: industry standard languages, verification methods, and EDA flow fit.

sourceAnchors.title: Lessons Learned 2023 page 20: Industry standard tools
sourceAnchors.claimSupported: The deck states that open-source hardware projects should use industry-standard languages and verification methods and fit existing EDA tool flows.

sourceAnchors.title: HiPEAC 2024 page 12: OpenHW deliverables
sourceAnchors.claimSupported: OpenHW deliverables include CORE-V core families, verification, documentation, compilers, RTOS/bare-metal/Linux support, FPGA images, SoCs, and commercial EDA tool compatibility.

content.speakerNotes: This slide is refreshingly practical. It says open-source hardware projects must fit the tool flows that companies already rely on. That does not weaken the open-source story; it makes the story usable. If an open core can be read publicly, improved collaboratively, and still pass through familiar industrial verification and implementation tools, it has a much better adoption path.

actions.text: This slide is refreshingly practical. It says open-source hardware projects must fit the tool flows that companies already rely on. That does not weaken the open-source story; it makes the story usable. If an open core can be read publicly, improved collaboratively, and still pass through familiar industrial verification and implementation tools, it has a much better adoption path.

scenes.title: The collaboration model turns contributions into lanes

content.title: The collaboration model turns contributions into lanes
content.subtitle: OpenHW is easier to understand when you see contribution areas as lanes rather than one giant community bucket.

steps.title: Cores
steps.body: Core families such as CVE2, CVE4, CVA6, and CVW need architecture decisions, RTL work, reviews, and maintenance.

steps.title: Verification
steps.body: CORE-V verification environments, tests, and methodology turn design claims into evidence.

steps.title: Software
steps.body: GCC, LLVM, RTOS, Linux, firmware, HAL, and IDE work make the cores usable by developers.

steps.title: Hardware and docs
steps.body: FPGA images, dev kits, documentation, and demos help users evaluate the IP in real systems.

sourceAnchors.title: HiPEAC 2024 page 14: How to collaborate
sourceAnchors.claimSupported: OpenHW collaboration is organized around cores, verification, documentation, hardware development, software development, and concrete demos/dev kits.

sourceAnchors.title: Lessons Learned 2023 page 23: SW Task Group
sourceAnchors.claimSupported: The SW Task Group supports toolchains, OS ports, firmware, GCC/LLVM, IDEs, FreeRTOS, HAL, and CORE-V MCU SDK work.

sourceAnchors.title: Lessons Learned 2023 page 25: HW Task Group
sourceAnchors.claimSupported: The HW Task Group supports SoC and FPGA evaluation/development platforms for cores and IP developed within OpenHW.

content.speakerNotes: OpenHW collaboration is not just, 'go contribute somewhere.' The materials show lanes: cores, verification, software, documentation, hardware development, boards, and demos. That matters for learners, because your contribution path depends on your strengths. An RTL engineer, a verification engineer, a compiler person, and a documentation-minded learner can all enter the ecosystem, but through different doors.

actions.text: OpenHW collaboration is not just, 'go contribute somewhere.' The materials show lanes: cores, verification, software, documentation, hardware development, boards, and demos. That matters for learners, because your contribution path depends on your strengths. An RTL engineer, a verification engineer, a compiler person, and a documentation-minded learner can all enter the ecosystem, but through different doors.

scenes.title: A contribution map should point to concrete artifacts

content.title: A contribution map should point to concrete artifacts
content.subtitle: The HiPEAC collaboration slide is rough visually, but the structure is exactly what a learning platform should preserve.
content.body: A useful OpenHW Explorer lesson should always answer: which repository, which document, which verification environment, which board, or which demo does this topic connect to?
content.caption: OpenHW how-to-collaborate slide, HiPEAC 2024, page 14.

callouts.title: From topic to artifact
callouts.body: The map links cores to verification, documentation, software, hardware development, and demos.

callouts.title: From artifact to course
callouts.body: This is why our course packages should preserve source anchors and not become generic lectures.

assets.caption: HiPEAC 2024 page 14: collaboration areas and concrete OpenHW artifacts.

sourceAnchors.title: HiPEAC 2024 page 14: How to collaborate
sourceAnchors.claimSupported: OpenHW collaboration is organized around cores, verification, documentation, hardware development, software development, and concrete demos/dev kits.

content.speakerNotes: This collaboration slide is useful for a very specific reason: it prevents vague community talk. It pushes us to name artifacts. Are we talking about a core repository, a verification environment, compiler work, an FPGA image, documentation, or a demo? For OpenHW Explorer, this is a design principle. Every classroom should help the learner move from idea to source, and from source to action.

actions.text: This collaboration slide is useful for a very specific reason: it prevents vague community talk. It pushes us to name artifacts. Are we talking about a core repository, a verification environment, compiler work, an FPGA image, documentation, or a demo? For OpenHW Explorer, this is a design principle. Every classroom should help the learner move from idea to source, and from source to action.

scenes.title: Roadmap and ecosystem make adoption less lonely

content.title: Roadmap and ecosystem make adoption less lonely
content.subtitle: The third adoption lesson is about what surrounds the IP after the first technical evaluation succeeds.
content.body: A dev kit is not only a board. It is a signal that cores, software, debug, silicon process, cloud connectivity, universities, members, and partners can coordinate around a concrete result.
content.caption: MCU DevKit slide, HiPEAC 2024, page 19.

callouts.title: IDE and software path
callouts.body: The 2023 deck also highlights CORE-V IDE, GCC, OpenOCD, examples, and getting-started material.

callouts.title: Board-level proof
callouts.body: FPGA emulation and dev kits reduce adoption uncertainty by giving engineers something concrete to run and inspect.

assets.caption: HiPEAC 2024 page 19: CORE-V MCU DevKit and multi-party coordination.

sourceAnchors.title: HiPEAC 2024 page 19: MCU DevKit
sourceAnchors.claimSupported: The CORE-V MCU DevKit is shown as a multi-party hardware result involving CV32E40P, QuickLogic eFPGA, GF 22FDX, debug, USB-C, AWS IoT connectivity, and several partners.

sourceAnchors.title: Lessons Learned 2023 page 23: SW Task Group
sourceAnchors.claimSupported: The SW Task Group supports toolchains, OS ports, firmware, GCC/LLVM, IDEs, FreeRTOS, HAL, and CORE-V MCU SDK work.

sourceAnchors.title: Lessons Learned 2023 page 25: HW Task Group
sourceAnchors.claimSupported: The HW Task Group supports SoC and FPGA evaluation/development platforms for cores and IP developed within OpenHW.

content.speakerNotes: The third adoption lesson is roadmap and ecosystem. A core can look impressive, but companies also ask what happens after evaluation. Is there an IDE path? Are there board images? Are there dev kits? Can the project coordinate universities, members, partners, debug, firmware, and silicon details? The MCU DevKit slide is a good example of ecosystem becoming tangible hardware.

actions.text: The third adoption lesson is roadmap and ecosystem. A core can look impressive, but companies also ask what happens after evaluation. Is there an IDE path? Are there board images? Are there dev kits? Can the project coordinate universities, members, partners, debug, firmware, and silicon details? The MCU DevKit slide is a good example of ecosystem becoming tangible hardware.

scenes.title: The larger argument: teachable, inspectable digital sovereignty

content.title: The larger argument: teachable, inspectable digital sovereignty
content.subtitle: The 2024 material connects permissive open source to know-how, education, innovation, and regional independence.
content.body: Open-source processor IP gives learners and companies a place to build knowledge instead of only consuming closed black boxes. That is one reason this website should be a learning navigator, not merely a link directory.
content.caption: Open source and digital sovereignty slide, HiPEAC 2024, page 16.

callouts.title: Education
callouts.body: Open source can be used to teach students because the implementation is inspectable.

callouts.title: Innovation
callouts.body: Permissive use allows adaptation, modification, production, and selling when the license path is understood.

assets.alt: Open source and digital sovereignty slide
assets.caption: HiPEAC 2024 page 16: permissive open source, education, innovation, and sovereignty.

sourceAnchors.title: HiPEAC 2024 page 16: Open source and digital sovereignty
sourceAnchors.claimSupported: The slide connects permissive open source with the ability to modify, innovate, produce, sell, build know-how, teach students, and support digital sovereignty.

content.speakerNotes: This is the slide that connects OpenHW to a broader mission. If processor IP is inspectable and permissively usable, students can learn from real implementation, companies can build internal know-how, and regions can reduce dependence on completely closed digital infrastructure. That does not magically make chip design easy. But it changes who is allowed to learn, inspect, improve, and build.

actions.text: This is the slide that connects OpenHW to a broader mission. If processor IP is inspectable and permissively usable, students can learn from real implementation, companies can build internal know-how, and regions can reduce dependence on completely closed digital infrastructure. That does not magically make chip design easy. But it changes who is allowed to learn, inspect, improve, and build.

scenes.title: What should you remember before the next course?

content.title: What should you remember before the next course?
content.subtitle: OpenHW is powerful because it is practical, but practical does not mean effortless.
takeaways: OpenHW complements RISC-V by organizing implementation-quality open-source processor IP and its supporting ecosystem.
Commercial adoption needs permissive licensing, credible verification, standard tool compatibility, and visible roadmap support.
CVA6 is the right deep-dive path for learning application-class open-source RISC-V core design with real system boundaries.
Open-source hardware still has hard constraints: specialized knowledge, tool cost, team coordination, and high chip investment.
content.nextStep: Continue into CVA6 From Zero when you want to move from ecosystem-level understanding to source-grounded architecture reading.
content.closing: A good OpenHW learner does not just collect links. They learn to connect mission, license, verification, roadmap, and source code into one engineering story.

sourceAnchors.title: HiPEAC 2024 page 20: Open source hardware issues
sourceAnchors.claimSupported: Open-source hardware still faces a smaller community, expensive third-party licenses, specialized knowledge needs, teamwork requirements, and high chip investment.

sourceAnchors.title: HiPEAC 2024 page 13: OpenHW RISC-V roadmap
sourceAnchors.claimSupported: The roadmap maps CVE2, CVE4, CVA6, CVW, and planned variants across performance classes, while noting that the roadmap is member-driven and may change.

sourceAnchors.title: Lessons Learned 2023 page 14: adoption lessons
sourceAnchors.claimSupported: The 2023 deck frames commercial open-source hardware adoption through permissive use, IP quality, and roadmap/ecosystem readiness.

content.speakerNotes: Before moving on, keep both sides of the story. OpenHW is exciting because it makes industrial-quality open-source RISC-V IP more inspectable and teachable. But the source material is honest: hardware communities are smaller than software communities, tools can still be expensive, special knowledge matters, and chip investment is high. That honesty is a strength. It tells us what a serious learning platform has to explain next.

actions.text: Before moving on, keep both sides of the story. OpenHW is exciting because it makes industrial-quality open-source RISC-V IP more inspectable and teachable. But the source material is honest: hardware communities are smaller than software communities, tools can still be expensive, special knowledge matters, and chip investment is high. That honesty is a strength. It tells us what a serious learning platform has to explain next.
